questions.question: Harshavardhana's empire was described by the Chinese pilgrim:
questions.explanation: Xuanzang (Hiuen Tsang) visited India during Harsha's reign (7th century CE) and described Harsha's empire and character in detail in his 'Si-Yu-Ki' (Record of Western Countries).

questions.question: Nalanda University was established during:
questions.explanation: Nalanda University was established during the Gupta period (5th century CE, under Kumaragupta I) and flourished until it was destroyed by Bakhtiyar Khilji in 1193 CE.

questions.question: Chandragupta II was also known as:
questions.explanation: Chandragupta II (375-415 CE) was known as Vikramaditya (Sun of Valor). His reign is considered the golden age of Gupta empire — Kalidasa, Varahamihira flourished in his court.

questions.question: 'Allahabad Pillar Inscription' (Prayag Prashasti) eulogizes:
questions.explanation: Prayag Prashasti (composed by court poet Harisena) on the Allahabad Pillar describes the military conquests and character of Samudragupta — called 'Napoleon of India'.

questions.question: The decimal system of numeration was developed in:
questions.explanation: The decimal system with place value notation (including zero) was developed in India during the Gupta period, later transmitted to Arabs and then to Europe.

questions.question: The Huna invasion of India weakened the:
questions.explanation: The Huna (Hephthalite) invasions in the late 5th and 6th centuries significantly weakened the Gupta Empire, contributing to its decline.

questions.question: Fa-Hien (Faxian) visited India during whose reign?
questions.explanation: Fa-Hien (399-414 CE), the Chinese Buddhist pilgrim, visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya) to collect Buddhist scriptures and study Buddhism.

questions.question: Kalidasa's famous works include:
questions.explanation: Kalidasa (Gupta period) wrote: Plays — Abhijnana Shakuntalam, Vikramorvasiyam, Malavikagnimitram; Epic poems — Raghuvamsa, Kumarasambhavam; Lyric poems — Meghaduta, Ritusamhara.

questions.question: Harshavardhana's Chinese visitor was:
questions.explanation: Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang), the Chinese Buddhist pilgrim, visited India during Harsha's reign (630-644 CE). His 'Si-Yu-Ki' gives valuable information about 7th century India.

questions.question: The Banabhatta was the court poet of:
questions.explanation: Banabhatta was the court poet of King Harshavardhana (606-647 CE) and wrote Harshacharita (biography of Harsha) and Kadambari (prose romance).

questions.question: The Gupta Empire is called the 'Golden Age' of India because:
options: It saw great achievements in science, mathematics, astronomy, literature, and art; Aryabhata, Kalidasa, iron pillar, decimal system
It had the most gold reserves
questions.explanation: The Gupta period (320–550 CE) is called the Golden Age: Aryabhata discovered zero and calculated pi; Kalidasa wrote Shakuntala, Meghaduta; Varahamihira was an astronomer; Sushruta was a physician; the Nalanda university flourished.

questions.question: Samudragupta is often called the 'Napoleon of India' because:
options: He conquered virtually all of northern India and received tribute from southern kingdoms through military campaigns
He introduced a new law code
questions.explanation: Samudragupta (c. 335–375 CE) is called the Napoleon of India by historian V.A. Smith for his extensive military conquests across North India and the Deccan. The Allahabad Pillar inscription (by Harishena) records his campaigns.

questions.question: Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya) defeated the:
options: Shakas (Western Kshatrapas) of Malwa and Gujarat
questions.explanation: Chandragupta II (c. 375–415 CE) defeated the Shakas (Western Kshatrapas) of western India, annexing Malwa, Gujarat, and Kathiawar. This extended Gupta empire to the Arabian Sea. He took the title 'Vikramaditya'.

questions.question: The Chinese pilgrim Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of:
questions.explanation: Fa-Hien (Faxian) visited India (399–414 CE) during Chandragupta II's reign. He described a prosperous, peaceful society, absence of capital punishment, and abundant food. He came to collect Buddhist scriptures.

questions.question: The Gupta period mathematician who wrote 'Aryabhatiya' was:
questions.explanation: Aryabhata (476–550 CE) wrote Aryabhatiya at age 23, containing mathematics and astronomy. He calculated pi ≈ 3.1416, explained solar eclipses, calculated Earth's rotation, and may have used a place-value system.

questions.question: The Pallava king who defeated the Chalukya king Pulakesi II was:
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I defeated Chalukya king Pulakesi II (who had previously defeated Harshavardhana) in 642 CE. He captured the Chalukya capital Vatapi and took the title 'Vatapikonda' (conqueror of Vatapi).

questions.question: The Gupta Empire was founded by which ruler?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta I is considered the founder of the Gupta Empire, establishing its rule in Magadha around 320 CE.

questions.question: Which Chinese pilgrim visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II?
questions.explanation: Fa-Hien (Faxian) visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II and recorded his observations on Gupta society and Buddhism.

questions.question: The famous Iron Pillar of Delhi belongs to the period of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The Iron Pillar of Delhi was erected during the reign of Chandragupta II, known for its rust-resistant composition.

questions.question: Harshavardhana shifted his capital from Thanesar to which city?
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana moved his capital to Kannauj, which became a major political and cultural center under his rule.

questions.question: Which Pallava king built the Shore Temple at Mamallapuram?
questions.explanation: The Shore Temple was built by Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha), a prominent Pallava ruler known for his architectural contributions.

questions.question: What was the capital of the Gupta Empire?
questions.explanation: Pataliputra (modern Patna) served as the capital of the Gupta Empire, continuing the Mauryan tradition.

questions.question: The Chinese traveller Hiuen Tsang visited India during the reign of which ruler?
questions.explanation: Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang) visited India during Harsha's reign and wrote detailed accounts of the political and religious conditions.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is known as the 'Napoleon of India' for his military conquests?
questions.explanation: Samudragupta earned the title 'Napoleon of India' due to his extensive military campaigns and expansion of the Gupta Empire.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta kingdom in Tamil Nadu was prominent during the 6th-7th centuries?

questions.question: The famous Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram was built by which Pallava ruler?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanatha temple was built by Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha), showcasing early Dravidian architecture.

questions.question: The Gupta Empire is known for a 'Golden Age' in which field?

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is known as 'Napoleon of India'?
questions.explanation: Samudragupta was a great conqueror, often called the Napoleon of India by historians.

questions.explanation: Nalanda was established in the Gupta period and continued to flourish in the post-Gupta (Pala) period.

questions.explanation: Sri Gupta was the first known ruler and founder of the Gupta dynasty.

questions.question: The Pallava dynasty is associated with which region?

questions.question: The Badami Chalukyas were contemporaries of which dynasty?

questions.explanation: The Dashavatara Temple at Deogarh is a Gupta period temple, an early example of Nagara architecture.

questions.question: The Nalanda University, a major center of learning, flourished during which period?

questions.question: The post-Gupta period saw the rise of which dynasty in the Kanchipuram region of Tamil Nadu?

questions.question: The famous rock-cut temples at Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram) were built by which dynasty?

questions.question: The Harsha Charita, a biography of King Harshavardhana, was written by?
questions.explanation: Banabhatta, the court poet of Harsha, wrote the Harsha Charita in Sanskrit.

questions.question: The Gupta period is often called the 'Golden Age' of ancient India primarily due to?
questions.explanation: The Gupta period witnessed remarkable progress in art, literature, science, and trade, earning the title 'Golden Age'.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta dynasty is associated with the famous Kailasanatha Temple at Ellora?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanatha Temple at Ellora was built by the Rashtrakuta king Krishna I in the 8th century.

questions.question: Who is considered the founder of the Gupta Empire?

questions.question: The Battle of Vatapi (Badami) was fought between which two rulers?

questions.question: The famous rock-cut temples at Mahabalipuram were built by which dynasty?

questions.question: Which Chinese pilgrim visited India during the reign of Harshavardhana?

questions.question: Which period is often referred to as the 'Golden Age of India'?
questions.explanation: The Gupta period (c. 4th–6th century CE) is known for its cultural, scientific, and economic prosperity, earning it the title 'Golden Age of India'.

questions.question: Who among the following Gupta rulers assumed the title 'Vikramaditya'?

questions.question: The Chinese pilgrim Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Fa-Hien traveled to India in the early 5th century AD, primarily during the reign of Chandragupta II.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for building the rock-cut temples at Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram)?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I, also called Mamalla, constructed the Rathas and mandapas at Mamallapuram.

questions.question: The 'Age of Imperial Kanauj' is associated with which post-Gupta ruler?

questions.question: The Gupta period is considered the 'Golden Age' of Indian history primarily due to advancements in:

questions.question: The Gupta period is often referred to as the 'Golden Age of India' primarily due to its achievements in which field?

questions.question: Which Chinese pilgrim visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II and left a detailed account of Gupta society?

questions.question: The famous Iron Pillar of Delhi, known for its rust-resistant composition, was erected during which period?

questions.question: Which post-Gupta ruler is credited with defeating the Hunas and assuming the title 'Maharajadhiraja'?

questions.question: The Nalanda University, a major center of learning, reached its peak under the patronage of which dynasty?

questions.question: Which Pallava ruler is known for constructing the rock-cut 'Ratha' temples at Mahabalipuram?

questions.question: The 'Land Grant' system, which led to the rise of feudalism in India, became prominent during which period?

questions.question: Which post-Gupta dynasty is associated with the construction of the famous Kailasa temple at Ellora?

questions.question: The 'Battle of Takkolam' (949 CE) was fought between the Cholas and which other South Indian kingdom?

questions.question: The 'Sangam' literature, though composed earlier, was compiled and codified during the early centuries of the Common Era under the patronage of which dynasty?

questions.question: The famous play 'Nagananda' was written by which post-Gupta ruler?

questions.question: Who was the first Gupta ruler to assume the title 'Maharajadhiraja'?

questions.question: The famous Iron Pillar of Mehrauli, known for its rust-resistant composition, was erected during which period?

questions.question: The 'Kailasanathar Temple' at Kanchipuram, built by Narasimhavarman II, is associated with which dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanathar Temple at Kanchipuram was built by the Pallava king Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) and is a fine example of early Dravidian architecture.

questions.question: The famous iron pillar of Mehrauli, known for its rust-resistant metallurgy, was erected during the rule of which Gupta ruler?

questions.question: The 'Pandya king' who is credited with ending the Kalabhra interregnum and reviving Tamil classical literature is:

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Gupta Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta I (c. 319-335 CE) is considered the founder of the Gupta Empire, establishing the dynasty's rule in Magadha.

questions.question: Samudragupta is often referred to as the 'Napoleon of India' by which historian?
questions.explanation: V.A. Smith, a British historian, coined the phrase 'Napoleon of India' for Samudragupta due to his extensive military conquests.

questions.question: The Gupta era (calendar) is traditionally dated from which year?
questions.explanation: The Gupta era began in 319-320 CE, corresponding to the accession of Chandragupta I, and was used for dating inscriptions.

questions.question: Which famous Sanskrit poet and playwright lived during the Gupta period?
questions.explanation: Kalidasa, the greatest Sanskrit poet and dramatist, flourished in the court of Chandragupta II (c. 4th-5th century CE).

questions.question: Harshavardhana, the post-Gupta ruler, had his capital at which city?
questions.explanation: Harsha shifted his capital from Thaneswar to Kannauj (Kanyakubja), which became a major political center in northern India.

questions.question: The Chalukya king Pulakesin II defeated Harsha on the banks of which river?
questions.explanation: Pulakesin II of the Chalukya dynasty repelled Harsha's southern campaign at the Narmada River, establishing the river as a boundary.

questions.question: Which Pallava king was a contemporary of Harshavardhana and known as 'Mamalla'?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I (c. 630-668 CE) was a Pallava king who defeated the Chalukyas and built the Mamallapuram (Mahabalipuram) monuments.

questions.question: The Vakataka queen Prabhavatigupta, who ruled as regent, was the daughter of which Gupta emperor?
questions.explanation: Prabhavatigupta was the daughter of Chandragupta II and married the Vakataka king Rudrasena II, later acting as regent for her sons.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major port and cultural center under the Pallavas during the post-Gupta period?
questions.explanation: Mamallapuram (Mahabalipuram) was a flourishing port city under the Pallavas, famous for its rock-cut temples and monuments.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is known for his military campaigns against the Hunas and for patronizing the Buddhist monk Vasubandhu?
questions.explanation: Skandagupta repelled the Hunas and is mentioned in the Junagadh inscription as supporting Vasubandhu.

questions.question: The famous iron pillar of Delhi, known for its rust-resistant composition, was erected during the reign of which early Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The iron pillar is attributed to Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya) and stands as a testament to Gupta metallurgy.

questions.question: Which Chinese pilgrim visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II and left detailed accounts of the Gupta empire?
questions.explanation: Faxian (Fa-hien) visited India around 399-414 CE during Chandragupta II's rule and wrote about Gupta society and Buddhism.

questions.question: The Gupta period is often called the 'Golden Age' of India due to advancements in which field?
questions.explanation: The Gupta age saw flourishing Sanskrit literature (Kalidas) and scientific works (Aryabhatta), earning the 'Golden Age' epithet.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta ruler of the Vardhana dynasty is remembered for his patronage of the Nalanda University and his play 'Nagananda'?
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana (606-647 CE) supported Nalanda and authored dramas like 'Nagananda' and 'Ratnavali'.

questions.question: The Chalukya king Pulakeshin II defeated Harshavardhana on the banks of which river, halting his southern expansion?
questions.explanation: Pulakeshin II repelled Harsha's army at the Narmada river, as recorded in the Aihole inscription.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is credited with the construction of the shore temple at Mamallapuram and the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) built the Shore Temple and the Kailasanatha temple, showcasing mature Pallava architecture.

questions.question: The 'Dakshinamurthy' image and the concept of 'Sapta Matrikas' gained prominence during the post-Gupta period under which dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Pallavas popularized the Dakshinamurthy (Shiva as guru) and Sapta Matrika cults in South Indian temple iconography.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta kingdom in Bengal was established by Shashanka and is known for opposing Harshavardhana?
questions.explanation: Shashanka founded the Gauda kingdom in Bengal and is noted for his rivalry with Harsha and the assassination of Rajyavardhana.

questions.question: The Pallava king Mahendravarman I was a patron of which language and wrote the play 'Mattavilasa Prahasana'?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I composed 'Mattavilasa Prahasana' in Sanskrit, reflecting his literary interests and religious tolerance.

questions.question: The Chinese traveller Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II (AD 405-411).

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is associated with the Iron Pillar inscription near Delhi?
questions.explanation: The Iron Pillar of Delhi was erected by Chandragupta II, commemorating his achievements.

questions.question: The famous astronomer Aryabhata belonged to which period?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata lived during the Gupta period (born AD 476), contributing significantly to mathematics and astronomy.

questions.question: Which Huna ruler invaded India during the declining phase of the Gupta Empire?
questions.explanation: Toramana led the Huna invasions that severely weakened the Gupta Empire in the 5th century.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta dynasty built the Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram?
questions.explanation: The Shore Temple was built by the Pallava king Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha).

questions.question: The Gupta period is often called the 'Golden Age' of India primarily due to achievements in which field?
questions.explanation: The Gupta period witnessed a flourishing of Sanskrit literature, sculpture, and temple architecture, earning it the title 'Golden Age'.

questions.question: The play 'Nagananda' was written by which post-Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana, the Vardhana king, authored three plays including 'Nagananda'.

questions.question: Which Chalukya king defeated Harshavardhana on the banks of the Narmada?
questions.explanation: Pulakesin II of the Chalukya dynasty checked Harsha's southward expansion in a battle on the Narmada.

questions.question: The iron pillar at Mehrauli is attributed to the reign of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The Mehrauli iron pillar bears an inscription indicating it was erected in honour of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya).

questions.question: Which Pallava ruler built the Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) constructed the Shore Temple, a UNESCO World Heritage site.

questions.question: The astronomer Aryabhata flourished during which period?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata lived in the Gupta era (c. 476 CE) and authored the Aryabhatiya.

questions.question: The Dasavatara temple at Deogarh was built during which dynasty?
questions.explanation: Deogarh's Dasavatara temple (c. 5th century CE) is a classic example of Gupta temple architecture.

questions.question: Which Gupta-era scholar authored the 'Brihat Samhita'?
questions.explanation: Varahamihira, a renowned astronomer in the court of Chandragupta II, wrote the encyclopedic 'Brihat Samhita'.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is known for issuing the largest number of gold coins and was called 'Kaviraja'?
questions.explanation: Samudragupta was a great patron of poetry and music, earning the title 'Kaviraja' and issuing many gold coins.

questions.question: The Iron Pillar of Delhi, known for its rust-resistant quality, belongs to which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The Iron Pillar was erected in memory of Chandragupta II, a prominent Gupta emperor.

questions.question: The post-Gupta period saw the rise of the Vardhana dynasty. Who was its most famous ruler?
questions.explanation: Harsha Vardhana ruled from 606-647 CE and was a notable post-Gupta ruler.

questions.question: Which Pallava ruler is credited with the construction of the Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram?
questions.explanation: The Shore Temple was built by Narasimhavarman II, also known as Rajasimha.

questions.question: The Aihole inscription of Pulakeshin II mentions his victory over Harsha. To which dynasty did Pulakeshin II belong?
questions.explanation: Pulakeshin II was a prominent ruler of the Badami Chalukya dynasty.

questions.question: The Gupta period is considered the 'Golden Age' of Indian history. Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of this period?
questions.explanation: The Gupta period saw flourishing trade, so decline of trade and commerce is not a characteristic.

questions.question: Who among the following was a notable astronomer and mathematician during the Gupta period?
questions.explanation: Aryabhata (born 476 CE) was a Gupta-era mathematician and astronomer.

questions.question: Nalanda University, a major center of learning, was established during the reign of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Nalanda University was founded by Kumaragupta I during the Gupta period.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta dynasty in South India is known for its cave temples at Badami, Aihole, and Pattadakal?
questions.explanation: The Chalukyas of Badami are renowned for their rock-cut and structural temples at these sites.

questions.question: Who is considered the founder of the Gupta dynasty?
questions.explanation: Sri Gupta (c. 240-280 CE) is regarded as the founder of the Gupta dynasty, though Chandragupta I was the first major ruler.

questions.question: During the reign of which Gupta ruler did the Chinese pilgrim Fa-Hien visit India?
questions.explanation: Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II Vikramaditya (c. 375-415 CE) and left valuable accounts of the Gupta period.

questions.question: What was the capital of Harshavardhana's empire?
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana (606-647 CE) moved his capital from Thanesar to Kannauj (Kanyakubja), which became the center of his vast empire.

questions.question: Which Chalukya king defeated the northern ruler Harsha?
questions.explanation: Pulakeshin II, the Chalukya king, defeated Harsha on the banks of the Narmada river around 630 CE, halting his southward expansion.

questions.question: The court of which Gupta ruler is famous for the 'Navaratnas' (nine gems)?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta II Vikramaditya's court was adorned by the Navaratnas, including Kalidasa, Amarasimha, and other luminaries.

questions.question: The Allahabad Pillar inscription (Prayag Prashasti) describes the achievements of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The inscription, composed by Harisena, details the military conquests and generous nature of Samudragupta.

questions.question: The Vakataka kingdom in central India was contemporary to which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta II married his daughter Prabhavatigupta to the Vakataka king, forging a strong alliance between the two dynasties.

questions.question: Which Pallava king built the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanatha temple was built by Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) in the 8th century and is a masterpiece of Pallava architecture.

questions.question: The play 'Nagananda' was written by which post-Gupta king?
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana, the king of Thanesar and Kannauj, authored three plays: Ratnavali, Nagananda, and Priyadarsika.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is known as the 'Indian Napoleon' for his military conquests?
questions.explanation: Samudragupta is called the 'Indian Napoleon' due to his extensive military campaigns and conquests across India.

questions.question: Who is the author of the Sanskrit play 'Abhijnanasakuntalam'?
questions.explanation: Kalidasa, the greatest poet of the Gupta period, wrote 'Abhijnanasakuntalam' (The Recognition of Shakuntala).

questions.question: Which Chalukya ruler successfully repulsed the invasion of Harshavardhana?
questions.explanation: Pulakeshin II of the Chalukya dynasty defeated Harsha on the banks of the Narmada, halting his southern expansion.

questions.question: The Iron Pillar of Mehrauli, known for its rust-resistant composition, is associated with which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The Mehrauli Iron Pillar bears an inscription of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya) and stands as a marvel of Gupta metallurgy.

questions.question: Which Pallava ruler is credited with building the Shore Temple at Mamallapuram?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) constructed the Shore Temple, a structural granite temple of the Pallava period.

questions.question: Nalanda University, a major centre of learning, was founded by which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Kumaragupta I founded Nalanda University, which later flourished as a renowned Buddhist educational institution.

questions.question: What was the capital of Harshavardhana's empire during the post-Gupta period?
questions.explanation: Harsha shifted his capital from Thanesar to Kannauj (Kanyakubja), making it the political centre of his empire.

questions.question: Which term refers to tax-free land grants given to Brahmins during the Gupta period?
questions.explanation: Agrahara were land grants to Brahmins, often exempted from taxes, and they played a key role in spreading Brahmanical culture.

questions.question: The court poet of Harshavardhana, who wrote 'Harshacharita', was:
questions.explanation: Banabhatta, the Sanskrit scholar, served as Harsha's court poet and authored the biographical work 'Harshacharita'.

questions.question: The Gupta ruler who is known as the 'Napoleon of India' is?
questions.explanation: Samudragupta was called the 'Napoleon of India' by historian V.A. Smith for his extensive military conquests.

questions.question: The famous 'Mehrauli Iron Pillar' is associated with which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The iron pillar at Mehrauli, Delhi, bears an inscription of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya).

questions.question: The court poet of Samudragupta who composed the Allahabad Pillar inscription is?
questions.explanation: Harisena was the court poet and minister of Samudragupta, and he composed the Allahabad Prashasti.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta ruler is known for his work 'Harshacharita' written by Banabhatta?
questions.explanation: Banabhatta wrote the 'Harshacharita' describing the life of King Harshavardhana of Kanauj.

questions.question: The Pallava king who built the Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram is?
questions.explanation: The Shore Temple at Mamallapuram was built by Narasimhavarman II, also known as Rajasimha, in the 8th century.

questions.question: The 'Aihole inscription' (634 CE) provides information about which Chalukya king?
questions.explanation: The Aihole inscription, composed by Ravikirti, eulogizes the achievements of Chalukya king Pulakesin II.

questions.question: The Udayagiri caves (Madhya Pradesh) contain inscriptions of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The Udayagiri caves have inscriptions and sculptures from the reign of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya).

questions.question: The post-Gupta period witnessed the rise of the Bhakti movement in South India. Which of the following is a group of Shaiva saints?
questions.explanation: Nayanars were the Shaiva saints of Tamil Nadu during the post-Gupta period, while Alvars were Vaishnava.

questions.question: Which Pallava ruler is credited with the construction of the 'Kailasanatha Temple' at Kanchipuram?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanatha Temple at Kanchipuram was built by Pallava king Rajasimha (Narasimhavarman II) in the early 8th century.

questions.question: The Gupta emperor who is considered the founder of the Gupta Empire and started the Gupta era (320 AD) was?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta I was the first ruler to assume the title 'Maharajadhiraja' and started the Gupta era in 320 AD, while Sri Gupta was the dynasty's founder but not the imperial founder.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is famously known as the 'Indian Napoleon' for his military conquests?
questions.explanation: Samudragupta is called the 'Napoleon of India' for his extensive military campaigns and conquests, as recorded in the Allahabad Pillar inscription.

questions.question: The famous Iron Pillar at Mehrauli, Delhi, which is rust-free, was erected during the reign of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The Mehrauli Iron Pillar bears an inscription attributing it to the reign of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya).

questions.question: The Chinese Buddhist pilgrim Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II and documented the prosperity of the Gupta empire.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is credited with the foundation of the Nalanda University?
questions.explanation: Nalanda University was founded by Kumaragupta I and later flourished under the support of later rulers including Harsha.

questions.question: The Pancha Rathas (Five Rathas) at Mahabalipuram are attributed to which Pallava ruler?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I, also known as Mamalla, built the Pancha Rathas and other monuments at Mahabalipuram.

questions.question: The Kailasanatha Temple at Kanchipuram was built by which Pallava ruler?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanatha Temple is a masterpiece of Pallava architecture built by Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) in the 8th century.

questions.question: Which early Chalukya ruler is famous for the Aihole inscription that gives details of his conquests and also mentions the Pallava king?
questions.explanation: The Aihole inscription (634 AD) of Pulakesin II describes his victories, including the defeat of Harsha and his conflict with the Pallavas.

questions.question: The Vikramashila University was founded by which ruler of the Pala dynasty?
questions.explanation: Dharmapala, the second ruler of the Pala dynasty, founded the Vikramashila University as a major Buddhist learning center.

questions.question: The Dashavatara Temple at Deogarh, known for its Gupta architecture, was built during the rule of which Gupta emperor?
questions.explanation: The Dashavatara Temple at Deogarh is a classic example of Gupta temple architecture, often attributed to the Gupta period under Chandragupta II.

questions.question: The famous Iron Pillar of Delhi, known for its rust-resistant composition, was originally erected during which period?
questions.explanation: The Iron Pillar of Delhi was erected during the Gupta period, specifically in honor of King Chandragupta II.

questions.question: The Nalanda University, a major center of learning, reached its peak under the patronage of which post-Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana (7th century) was a great patron of Nalanda University, which attracted scholars from across Asia.

questions.question: Which Pallava ruler is known for constructing the Shore Temple at Mamallapuram, a UNESCO World Heritage site?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) built the Shore Temple and other structural temples at Mamallapuram in the 8th century.

questions.question: The 'Battle of Pullalur' (618-619 CE) was fought between which two powers?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Pullalur was fought between the Pallava king Narasimhavarman I and the Chalukya king Pulakeshin II.

questions.question: The Gupta period is often referred to as the 'Golden Age of Indian culture'. Which of the following is NOT a correct characteristic of this period?
options: Revival of Vaishnavism and the composition of the Puranas
Significant development in Sanskrit literature with Kalidasa
Beginning of the construction of monolithic rathas at Mahabalipuram
Issuance of the largest number of gold coins in ancient India
questions.explanation: Monolithic rathas at Mahabalipuram were built during the Pallava period under Narasimhavarman I, not during the Gupta period.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta dynasty, ruling from the Coromandel coast, is credited with the construction of the Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram and the introduction of the Dravidian style of architecture?
questions.explanation: The Pallava dynasty, particularly under Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha), built the Shore Temple and pioneered Dravidian rock-cut and structural architecture.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Gupta period is/are correct? 1. The practice of land grants to Brahmins and monasteries began. 2. The Huns under Toramana and Mihirakula invaded during the later Gupta period. 3. The Nalanda University was established by Kumaragupta I.
questions.explanation: Land grants began in Gupta period; Huns invaded later; Nalanda was founded by Kumaragupta I, but the statement is correct.

questions.question: Consider the following pairs: 1. Varahamihira - Brihat Samhita, 2. Kalidasa - Meghaduta, 3. Aryabhata - Surya Siddhanta. Which of these pairs is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Varahamihira wrote Brihat Samhita; Kalidasa wrote Meghaduta; Aryabhata wrote Aryabhatiya, not Surya Siddhanta.

questions.question: The post-Gupta period saw the rise of the Vardhana dynasty. Which of the following statements about Harshavardhana is incorrect?
options: He was a patron of Nalanda University
His court poet Banabhatta wrote Harshacharita
He defeated the Chalukya king Pulakesin II
questions.explanation: Harsha was defeated by Pulakesin II on the banks of Narmada, not the other way.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major feature of the Gupta administration that distinguished it from the Mauryan system?
questions.explanation: Gupta administration relied on feudatories and land grants, unlike Mauryan centralization.

questions.question: The 'Golden Age' of Gupta period is often debated. Which of the following statements supports the view that it was not a golden age for all?
options: It saw decline in trade with Rome
It had no major architectural innovation
questions.explanation: Trade decline, caste rigidity, and limited architecture (compared to later) challenge the 'golden age' label.

questions.question: Consider the following about post-Gupta kingdoms: 1. The Maitrakas of Valabhi issued silver coins. 2. The Maukharis controlled Kannauj. 3. The Gaudas of Bengal were contemporaries of Harsha. Which are correct?
questions.explanation: All three are correct: Maitrakas issued silver coins; Maukharis ruled Kannauj; Gaudas were contemporary to Harsha.

questions.question: Which of the following Gupta rulers is credited with the first known land grant to a Buddhist monastery?
questions.explanation: Kumaragupta I's Damodarpur copper plate records a land grant to a Buddhist vihara.

questions.question: The post-Gupta period saw the emergence of the 'Tripartite Struggle'. Which of the following correctly describes the main contenders?
questions.explanation: The Tripartite Struggle was between the Pratiharas, Palas, and Rashtrakutas for control of Kannauj.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Gupta economy is correct?
options: Silk was a major export to Rome
Iron metallurgy declined after Gupta period
Land revenue was fixed at one-sixth of produce
questions.explanation: Silk export to Rome, post-Gupta iron decline, and one-sixth land revenue are all correct.

questions.question: The 'Aihole inscription' of Pulakesin II mentions which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: The Aihole inscription of Pulakesin II mentions Samudragupta as a ruler who was defeated by the Chalukyas.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Gupta period:
1. The Allahabad Pillar inscription of Samudragupta mentions the payment of tribute by the rulers of south India.
2. The Gupta kings assumed the title 'Maharajadhiraja' to indicate their paramount sovereignty.
3. The land grants to religious beneficiaries began on a large scale during the Gupta period.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: All three are correct. The Allahabad Pillar lists southern kings who paid tribute; 'Maharajadhiraja' was common; and land grants to Brahmins and temples proliferated.

questions.question: With reference to the post-Gupta period, which of the following correctly matches the dynasty with its contemporary kingdom or ruler?
questions.explanation: The Vakatakas were contemporaries of the Guptas; Samudragupta's inscriptions mention Vakataka king. Other pairs are mismatched: Pallavas vs Chalukyas; Harsha vs Pulakeshin; Kadambas are earlier.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the position of women in the Gupta period is NOT correct?
options: Women could inherit property under certain conditions
The practice of Sati was prevalent among some sections
Women could receive higher education and were allowed to choose their husbands
The Devadasi system had started in the Gupta period
questions.explanation: The Devadasi system became prominent only in the medieval period. Gupta women could inherit (Stridhana), Sati existed, and women like Gargi were educated; but freedom in mate choice was limited.

questions.question: The 'Harshacharita' and the 'Aryabhatiya' are two important literary works belonging to the same broad period. Who are their respective authors?
questions.explanation: Banabhatta wrote Harshacharita (biography of Harsha), and Aryabhata wrote Aryabhatiya (mathematical treatise). Both belong to the early post-Gupta period (6th-7th century CE).

questions.question:
Consider the following pairs of post-Gupta kingdoms and their capitals:
1. Pallavas – Kanchipuram
2. Chalukyas of Badami – Vatapi
3. Pandyas – Madurai
4. Western Ganga – Talakad
Which of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: All pairs are correct. Kanchipuram was Pallava capital; Badami (Vatapi) for Chalukyas; Madurai for Pandyas; Talakad for Western Gangas (post-Gupta period).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Gupta coinage is correct?
options: Gold coins were called 'Dinaara' and silver coins 'Rupyaka'
Gupta coins only depicted the king on the obverse and Goddess Lakshmi on the reverse
The earliest Gupta coins were minted by Chandragupta I in commemoration of his marriage
Gupta coinage had no influence of earlier Kushana or Roman designs
questions.explanation: Gold coins were called Dinara (derived from Roman denarius) and silver Rūpyaka. Coins often showed the king with a goddess (not only Lakshmi); marriage coins were issued by Samudragupta, not Chandragupta I; Kushana and Roman influence is evident.

questions.question: The famous Tamil saint Tirumangai Alvar is associated with the bhakti movement in the post-Gupta period. Which dynasty ruled the region where he primarily preached?
questions.explanation: Tirumangai Alvar (8th century) lived during the Pallava period and was involved in building temples like the one at Srirangam. Pallavas were the dominant power in Tamil Nadu during that time.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a feature of the 'Feudalism' that emerged in India during the post-Gupta period?
options: Grant of land to officials in lieu of salary
Subinfeudation of land by grant recipients
Centralized control of trade and guilds
Decrease in the use of coined money
questions.explanation: Post-Gupta feudalism saw land grants and subinfeudation, and a decline in coin use due to local economies, but guilds and trade became more localized, not centrally controlled.

questions.question: The Chinese traveler Hiuen Tsang visited India during the reign of Harsha and provided detailed accounts. Which of the following statements about his observations is correct?
options: He noted that the Pallava king Narasimhavarman I was a contemporary of Harsha
He mentions the decline of Buddhism in the Gangetic plains
He recorded the existence of a university at Nalanda with thousands of students
He stated that the Gupta empire was still intact in the 7th century
questions.explanation: Hiuen Tsang (c. 630-645 CE) praised Nalanda university. Pallava king Narasimhavarman I was later; Buddhism declined but still present; the Gupta empire had fallen by then.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the cultural developments in the Gupta and post-Gupta periods:
1. The rock-cut caves at Ajanta were entirely executed during the Gupta period.
2. The 'Dashakumaracharita' is a work of prose fiction by Dandin, a Pallava court poet.
3. The Tamil bhakti movement of the Nayanars and Alvars began in the post-Gupta period.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect: Ajanta caves span from 2nd century BCE to 7th century CE, with many executed in the Vakataka period (post-Gupta). Statements 2 (Dandin, Pallava court) and 3 (early medieval bhakti) are correct.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the Gupta period:
1. The official language of the Gupta court was Sanskrit.
2. The Allahabad Pillar inscription describes Samudragupta as 'Kaviraja'.
3. The Gupta king Chandragupta I assumed the title 'Maharajadhiraja'.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: All three statements are correct. Gupta inscriptions were in Sanskrit, Samudragupta was called Kaviraja, and Chandragupta I was the first to use Maharajadhiraja.

questions.question: Which of the following southern kingdoms is NOT mentioned in the Allahabad Pillar inscription of Samudragupta as having been conquered by him during his Dakshinapatha campaign?
questions.explanation: The inscription lists Kosala, Kalinga, Vengi, etc., but not the Pallava kingdom, which was independent at the time.

questions.question: The 'Tiger-slayer' type of gold coins were issued by which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Kumaragupta I issued coins depicting him as a tiger slayer, a distinctive type among Gupta coinage.

questions.question: Which of the following was the capital of Harshavardhana's empire?
questions.explanation: Harsha initially ruled from Thanesar but later moved his capital to Kannauj, which became the center of his empire.

questions.question: The Aihole inscription of Pulakesi II mentions his victory over which northern king?
questions.explanation: The Aihole inscription records Pulakesi II's defeat of Harshavardhana on the banks of the Narmada river.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is credited with the construction of the rock-cut cave temples at Mahabalipuram (Mamallapuram)?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I pioneered rock-cut architecture; his son Narasimhavarman I built the monolithic rathas later.

questions.question: The Huna invasion that posed a serious threat to the Gupta empire was successfully repelled by which ruler?
questions.explanation: Skandagupta defeated the Hunas around 455 AD, temporarily stabilizing the Gupta empire after the invasion.

questions.question: Which of the following scholars is traditionally considered one of the 'Navaratna' (nine gems) in the court of Chandragupta II but historically lived much later?
questions.explanation: Varahamihira (c. 505-587 AD) is often listed in the Navaratna but his floruit is after Chandragupta II's reign.

questions.question: Who among the following South Indian rulers was a contemporary of the Gupta emperor Samudragupta?
questions.explanation: Mayurasharma, founder of the Kadamba dynasty, ruled around 345–365 AD, overlapping with Samudragupta's reign (335–375 AD).

questions.question: The Vakataka queen Prabhavatigupta was the daughter of which Gupta emperor?
questions.explanation: Prabhavatigupta, daughter of Chandragupta II, married Vakataka king Rudrasena II and acted as regent for her sons.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler is known for adopting the title 'Maharajadhiraja' and issuing the first gold coins of the Gupta dynasty?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta I was the first Gupta ruler to assume the title Maharajadhiraja and issued gold coins, marking the beginning of the Gupta era in 320 CE.

questions.question: The Allahabad Pillar inscription provides details about the military campaigns of which Gupta emperor?
questions.explanation: The Allahabad Pillar inscription (Prayag Prashasti), composed by Harisena, enumerates the conquests and achievements of Samudragupta.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta dynasty in Tamil Nadu is credited with the construction of the Shore Temple at Mamallapuram?
questions.explanation: The Shore Temple was built by the Pallava king Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha) in the 8th century CE at Mamallapuram.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Gupta period: I. The Chinese pilgrim Fa-Hien visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II. II. The Nalanda University was founded during the Gupta period. III. The Gupta period is known as the 'Golden Age' of Indian art and literature. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: All three statements are correct: Fa-Hien visited during Chandragupta II, Nalanda was founded in the Gupta period, and the era is often called the Golden Age.

questions.question: Which post-Gupta work is attributed to Banabhatta, a court poet of King Harshavardhana?
questions.explanation: Kadambari is a prose romance written by Banabhatta, who was a court poet of Harshavardhana during the post-Gupta period.

questions.question: The Brihadishwara Temple at Thanjavur was built by which Chola king?
questions.explanation: Rajaraja Chola I constructed the Brihadishwara Temple (Peruvudaiyar Kovil) in Thanjavur in 1010 CE.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for the rock-cut temples at Mahabalipuram, including the 'Descent of the Ganges'?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I (Mamalla) is credited with the rock-cut monuments at Mahabalipuram, including the Descent of the Ganges (Arjuna's Penance).

questions.question: The Gupta era began in which year?
questions.explanation: The Gupta era commenced in 320 CE with the accession of Chandragupta I, marking the foundation of the Gupta dynasty.

questions.question: Which Gupta ruler's Bhitari pillar inscription records a victory over the Hunas?
questions.explanation: Skandagupta's Bhitari pillar inscription describes his successful defense against the Huna invasion, repelling the invaders.

questions.question: Which Pallava king defeated the Chalukya ruler Pulakesi II?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I avenged his father Mahendravarman I's defeat by invading and sacking the Chalukya capital Vatapi, defeating Pulakesi II.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Gupta period is correct?
options: The Huns under Toramana and Mihirakula completely destroyed the Gupta Empire by 500 CE.
The Gupta administration was highly centralized with no role for provincial governors.
The practice of land grants to Brahmins and Buddhist monks began on a large scale during this period.
The Gupta kings followed a policy of religious intolerance towards Jainism and Buddhism.
questions.explanation: Land grants (agrahara) to religious beneficiaries became widespread under the Guptas, marking a shift in polity and economy.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the post-Gupta period: (1) The Vardhana king Harsha shifted his capital from Thanesar to Kannauj. (2) The Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang visited India during Harsha's reign. (3) Harsha convened the famous Buddhist council at Nalanda. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Harsha moved his capital to Kannauj and Xuanzang visited his court; the Buddhist council was held at Kannauj, not Nalanda.

questions.question: The 'Three Crowned Kings' of the Sangam age are often contrasted with the post-Gupta Chalukya and Pallava kingdoms. Which of the following best describes a key difference in their administrative structure?
options: Sangam kings had a standing army; Chalukyas relied solely on feudal levies.
Pallavas introduced the concept of 'mandala' as a political unit; Sangam kingdoms used 'nadus'.
Chalukya and Pallava inscriptions are mostly in Prakrit; Sangam texts are in Tamil.
Sangam polities were primarily tribal monarchies; post-Gupta kingdoms had a more bureaucratic revenue system.
questions.explanation: Sangam kingdoms were clan-based with limited bureaucracy, while post-Gupta states like the Pallavas developed a structured land revenue and administrative hierarchy.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of Gupta-era scholars and their works is correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Varahamihira authored the Brihat Samhita; Mrichchhakatika is by Shudraka, Panchasiddhantika is by Varahamihira, and Mudrarakshasa is by Vishakhadatta.

questions.question: The post-Gupta period saw the rise of the 'Samanta' system. Which of the following best describes its impact on the political structure?
options: It strengthened the central authority by creating loyal military governors.
It led to the decentralization of power and the emergence of feudal lords.
It abolished the caste system by promoting merit-based appointments.
It introduced a uniform currency system across the subcontinent.
questions.explanation: The Samanta system involved subordinate rulers who often became autonomous, leading to political fragmentation and feudalism.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Pallava king Mahendravarman I is incorrect?
options: He was a patron of architecture and built the rock-cut temples at Mahabalipuram.
He was defeated by the Chalukya king Pulakeshin II.
He converted from Jainism to Shaivism under the influence of Appar.
questions.explanation: The rock-cut temples at Mahabalipuram were primarily built by his son Narasimhavarman I, not Mahendravarman I.

questions.question: Consider the following about the Gupta coinage: (1) The Gupta kings issued the largest number of gold coins in ancient India. (2) The 'Lion-slayer' type coin was issued by Samudragupta. (3) The 'Lyrist' type coin depicts Chandragupta II playing a veena. Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Gupta gold coinage was extensive; Samudragupta issued the 'Lion-slayer' type; the 'Lyrist' type depicts Samudragupta, not Chandragupta II.

questions.question: The post-Gupta period witnessed the rise of the 'Vakataka' kingdom. Which of the following is a notable contribution of the Vakatakas to Indian culture?
options: They built the Kailasa temple at Ellora.
They patronized the Ajanta cave paintings during the reign of Harishena.
They introduced the practice of 'Sati' in the Deccan.
They were the first to issue silver coins in South India.
questions.explanation: The Vakataka king Harishena patronized the Ajanta caves, which contain some of the finest Buddhist murals.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the Gupta Empire and the Tamil region?
options: The Guptas directly ruled the Tamil country as a province.
Samudragupta's southern campaign reached up to Kanchipuram but did not annex it.
The Pallavas were a vassal state of the Guptas.
The Gupta navy controlled the Bay of Bengal trade routes from Tamil ports.
questions.explanation: Samudragupta's Allahabad Pillar inscription mentions his conquest of southern kings up to Kanchipuram, but he did not annex the Tamil region.

questions.question: The 'Land Grant' system of the post-Gupta period is often linked to the decline of trade. Which of the following best explains this connection?
options: Land grants led to the hoarding of gold coins, reducing money supply.
Grants to Brahmins exempted land from taxes, reducing state revenue and trade infrastructure.
The system encouraged the growth of guilds that monopolized trade.
Land grants promoted the use of barter, eliminating the need for coinage.
questions.explanation: Tax-free land grants reduced royal income, weakening the state's ability to maintain trade routes and urban centers, leading to commercial decline.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the Gupta administration:
1. The king was assisted by a council of ministers called 'Mantriparishad'.
2. The empire was divided into provinces called 'Bhuktis' under 'Uparikas'.
3. The village headman was known as 'Gramika' or 'Gramadhipati'.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: All three statements are historically accurate regarding Gupta administrative structure.

questions.question:
Which of the following factors contributed to the decline of the Gupta Empire?
1. Huna invasions
2. Rise of feudatories and provincial governors
3. Trade decline and economic crisis
4. Weak successors after Skandagupta
Select the correct answer using the codes below:
questions.explanation: All four factors – Hun invasions, feudatory rise, economic decline, and weak successors – contributed to the Gupta decline.

questions.question: The Chinese pilgrim Hiuen Tsang visited India during the reign of which post-Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Hiuen Tsang visited India during the reign of Harsha (606–647 CE) and spent time at his court.

questions.question: The Chalukya king Pulakeshin II defeated which ruler on the banks of the Narmada river?
questions.explanation: Pulakeshin II defeated Harsha on the Narmada, halting Harsha's southern expansion.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best characterizes the Gupta period as the 'Golden Age'?
options: A. It was a period of extensive territorial expansion and military conquests.
B. It witnessed remarkable achievements in art, literature, science, and trade.
C. It saw the establishment of a centralized bureaucratic state.
D. It was marked by the complete absence of foreign invasions.
questions.explanation: The Gupta period is called the Golden Age due to unparalleled progress in culture, science, and economy.

questions.question:
Arrange the following post-Gupta rulers in chronological order:
1. Harsha
2. Pulakeshin II
3. Mahendravarman I
4. Narasimhavarman I
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I (c.600), Harsha (606), Pulakeshin II (610), Narasimhavarman I (630) – correct order is 3,1,2,4.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the post-Gupta period:
1. The practice of land grants to Brahmins and temples increased, leading to the rise of feudalism.
2. The Pallavas were patrons of Sanskrit and Prakrit literature.
3. The Chalukyas of Badami built the rock-cut temples at Ellora.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct; the Ellora temples were built by Rashtrakutas, not Chalukyas.

questions.question: The 'Lion-slayer' type gold coins were issued by which Gupta ruler?
questions.explanation: Samudragupta issued the 'Lion-slayer' type gold coins depicting him killing a lion.

questions.question: Which of the following post-Gupta dynasties is associated with the construction of the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram?
questions.explanation: The Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram was built by the Pallava king Narasimhavarman II (Rajasimha).

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about the Gupta period is/are correct?
1. The Prayaga Prashasti was composed by Harisena.
2. The Nalanda University was founded by Kumaragupta I.
3. The Mehrauli Iron Pillar inscription mentions Chandragupta II.
4. The Gupta era began with the accession of Sri Gupta.
questions.explanation: Prayaga Prashasti by Harisena, Nalanda founded by Kumaragupta I, and Mehrauli pillar mentions Chandragupta II are correct; Gupta era began with Chandragupta I, not Sri Gupta.

questions.question:
Consider the following pairs of post-Gupta rulers and their titles:
1. Harshavardhana – Siladitya
2. Pulakeshin II – Satyashraya
3. Narasimhavarman I – Vatapikonda
4. Yasovarman – Paramabhattaraka
Which of the pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: All pairs are correct: Harsha was Siladitya, Pulakeshin II was Satyashraya, Narasimhavarman I was Vatapikonda, and Yasovarman was Paramabhattaraka.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Chalukya ruler Pulakeshin II is incorrect?
options: He defeated Harshavardhana on the banks of the Narmada River.
He sent an embassy to the Persian king Khusrau II.
He was defeated and killed by the Pallava king Narasimhavarman I.
He performed the Aswamedha sacrifice to assert his sovereignty.
questions.explanation: Pulakeshin II did not perform Aswamedha; he assumed the title 'Satyashraya' and was a Vaishnava. Other statements are historically correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The Gupta period is often called the 'Golden Age' of ancient India.
Reason (R): The Guptas achieved remarkable progress in science, literature, art, and administration, with minimal foreign threats.
Select the correct answer:
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.
questions.explanation: Both are true; the Gupta period saw advancements in fields like astronomy (Aryabhata), literature (Kalidasa), and stable administration, justifying the 'Golden Age' label.

questions.question: The Chinese traveller Hiuen Tsang visited India during the reign of Harshavardhana. Which of the following observations made by him is NOT accurate?
options: He noted the existence of a strong university at Nalanda.
He recorded that the Kshatriyas were the highest varna in the social hierarchy.
He described the city of Kanauj as prosperous and well-fortified.
He mentioned that the king Harsha patronised both Buddhism and Hinduism.
questions.explanation: Hiuen Tsang recorded that Brahmins were the highest varna, not Kshatriyas. The other observations are accurate.

questions.question: Which of the following post-Gupta dynasties was the first to issue gold coins in South India, and what was their primary script?
questions.explanation: The Pallavas were the first South Indian dynasty to issue gold coins, and they used the Grantha script for inscriptions and coin legends.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the Gupta administration:
1. The king was assisted by a council of ministers called 'Mantriparishad'.
2. The empire was divided into provinces called 'Bhuktis'.
3. Land revenue was fixed at one-sixth of the produce.
4. The 'Kumaramatyas' were provincial governors.
Which of these statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Statements 1, 2, and 3 are correct; Kumaramatyas were high-ranking officials, not specifically provincial governors—that role was held by 'Uparikas'.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the decline of the Gupta Empire?
options: Continuous invasions by the Huns under Toramana and Mihirakula.
The rise of feudatories like the Vardhanas and Maukharis.
Economic decline due to excessive donations to Brahmins.
All of the above contributed to the decline.
questions.explanation: All three factors—Huna invasions, feudal fragmentation, and economic strain from land grants—collectively led to the Gupta decline.

questions.question: The 'Dakshinapatha' route during the post-Gupta period was most closely associated with which of the following?
options: Trade between the Gupta heartland and the Deccan.
The spread of Buddhism from Magadha to Sri Lanka.
questions.explanation: Dakshinapatha was a major trade route connecting North India (Gupta region) to the Deccan, facilitating commerce and cultural exchange.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of post-Gupta rulers and their literary contributions is correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana wrote the play Nagananda; Bhavabhuti wrote Malatimadhava, Banabhatta wrote Harshacharita, and Kadambari is by Banabhatta, not Bhavabhuti.

questions.question: The Gupta period is often called the 'Golden Age of India'. Which of the following statements best critiques this characterization from a post-Gupta perspective?
options: It ignores the significant economic decline and loss of trade routes under the later Guptas.
It overlooks the cultural and architectural achievements of the Vakatakas and Chalukyas.
It fails to account for the superior administrative centralization achieved by the Mauryas.
It overemphasizes Sanskrit literature while neglecting Tamil Sangam works.
questions.explanation: The 'Golden Age' label often sidelines the parallel and subsequent cultural efflorescence of the Vakatakas (cave temples) and early Chalukyas (Aihole), who were contemporaries and successors.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the post-Gupta period: (i) The practice of 'Sati' became more widespread. (ii) The 'Varna' system became more rigid. (iii) Land grants to Brahmins increased. Which of these statements is/are corroborated by contemporary inscriptions and accounts like those of Hiuen Tsang?
questions.explanation: Hiuen Tsang's accounts note the prevalence of Sati, rigid caste hierarchies, and the proliferation of land grants (agrahara) to Brahmins, all of which intensified in the post-Gupta period.

questions.question: The 'Tripartite Struggle' for Kannauj involved the Palas, Pratiharas, and Rashtrakutas. What was the primary geopolitical consequence of this prolonged conflict for the Tamil region?
options: It led to the direct invasion of the Pandya kingdom by the Rashtrakutas.
It allowed the Cholas under Vijayalaya to capture Thanjavur from the Muttaraiyars.
It forced the Pallavas to shift their capital from Kanchipuram to Mamallapuram.
It resulted in the decline of the Chera monopoly over the spice trade.
questions.explanation: The distraction of the dominant powers in the Deccan and North India during the Tripartite Struggle provided the nascent Chola chief Vijayalaya the opportunity to capture Thanjavur (c. 850 CE) from the Muttaraiyar chieftains.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of a Gupta-era institution and its post-Gupta transformation is correctly matched?
options: Gupta 'Sreni' guilds - Post-Gupta 'Nagaram' assemblies of the Cholas
Gupta 'Agrahara' grants - Post-Gupta 'Devadana' grants to temples
questions.explanation: While Agraharas were land grants to Brahmins, the post-Gupta period saw a rise in Devadana (grants to temples), reflecting a shift from Brahmin-centric to temple-centric land patronage.

questions.question: A historian argues that the 'feudalism' debate in Indian history is most relevant for the post-Gupta period. Which of the following pieces of evidence would most strongly support the existence of a 'feudal' order in this era?
options: The use of the 'Saka' era in inscriptions.
The proliferation of 'Samantas' and 'Mahasamantas' as subordinate rulers.
The construction of monolithic rathas at Mamallapuram.
The compilation of the 'Kamasutra' by Vatsyayana.
questions.explanation: The rise of Samantas (feudatory chiefs) who received land grants in lieu of service and exercised local sovereignty is a key indicator of political decentralization and a feudal-like hierarchy in post-Gupta India.

questions.question: Consider the following about the Gupta and post-Gupta coinage: (i) Gupta gold coins (Dinars) were of high purity. (ii) Post-Gupta coinage saw a decline in gold content. (iii) The 'Varaha' coinage of the Vijaynagara empire was a revival of Gupta standards. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Gupta gold coins were renowned for purity. Post-Gupta, gold coinage declined in purity and volume, with silver and copper becoming more common. Vijayanagara's gold coinage was a later development, not a direct revival of Gupta standards.

questions.question: The 'Pallava-Ganga' conflict is a recurring theme in post-Gupta Tamil history. What was the primary strategic objective of the Pallavas in their wars against the Western Gangas?
options: To gain control over the pearl fisheries of the Gulf of Mannar.
To secure the fertile Kaveri delta region from Ganga incursions.
To dominate the trade route to the west coast via the Palghat gap.
To enforce the payment of tribute from the Ganga capital at Talakad.
questions.explanation: The Pallavas, especially under Narasimhavarman I, fought the Western Gangas (allies of the Chalukyas) to assert suzerainty and extract tribute, as the Gangas controlled the strategic region of southern Karnataka.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the 'Bhakti movement' in the post-Gupta Tamil region is incorrect?
options: The Nayanmars (Shaiva saints) and Alwars (Vaishnava saints) composed devotional hymns in Tamil.
The movement explicitly rejected the authority of the Vedas and caste hierarchy.
It was patronized by the Pallava and Pandya kings who built temples for the saints.
The Tevaram and Divya Prabandham are canonical collections of these hymns.
questions.explanation: While the Bhakti movement emphasized personal devotion and was open to all castes, it did not explicitly reject the Vedas; instead, it often sought to reinterpret Vedic principles through vernacular devotion.

questions.question: The Gupta period saw the development of the 'Navaratnas' (nine gems) at the court of Chandragupta II. Which of the following post-Gupta courts is most analogous in its role as a major center of literary and cultural patronage?
options: The court of Harshavardhana at Kannauj.
The court of the Rashtrakuta king Amoghavarsha I.
The court of the Pandya king Nedumaran (Koon Pandiyan).
The court of the Chola king Rajaraja I at Thanjavur.
questions.explanation: Amoghavarsha I's court was a major literary center, patronizing scholars like Jinasena and Mahaviracharya, and he himself authored the 'Kavirajamarga', a seminal work on poetics in Kannada.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the 'Land Revenue System' in the Gupta and post-Gupta periods: (i) The Gupta state collected a standard 1/6th of the produce as tax. (ii) Post-Gupta, the 'Bhaga' tax was often collected by intermediaries. (iii) The 'Udranga' tax was a type of water tax. Which of these statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Gupta state's standard share was 1/6th (Shadbhaga). Post-Gupta, the Bhaga (king's share) was often collected by Samantas. Udranga was a tax on permanent tenants, often linked to irrigation or land use.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the decline of the Gupta Empire is incorrect?
options: The Hun invasions under Toramana and Mihirakula weakened the empire.
The Gupta feudatories like the Pushyabhutis and Maukharis asserted independence.
The Gupta Empire collapsed immediately after Skandagupta's death with no successors.
The decline led to the rise of regional powers such as the Vakatakas and the later Guptas.
questions.explanation: Skandagupta was succeeded by later Gupta rulers like Purugupta and Kumaragupta II, so the empire did not collapse immediately.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding Harshavardhana: (1) He adopted Buddhism after the Kanauj assembly. (2) His administration was highly centralized with no feudatories. (3) He was defeated by Pulakeshin II on the banks of the Narmada. Which is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Harsha's administration had semi-autonomous feudatories, so statement 2 is false. He indeed adopted Buddhism after the assembly and was defeated by the Chalukya king Pulakeshin II.

questions.question: The post-Gupta period saw the emergence of the 'Samanta' system. Which of the following best describes its role in early medieval polity?
options: A land revenue official reporting directly to the king.
A feudatory chief who served as a military subordinate and collected local taxes.
A merchant guild head responsible for urban trade regulations.
A Buddhist monastic administrator controlling temple lands.
questions.explanation: Samantas were subordinate rulers who owed allegiance to the emperor, maintained troops, and collected revenue from their assigned territories.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of kingdoms/empires in the post-Gupta period is correctly matched with their capitals?
questions.explanation: All three pairs are correct: Harsha's capital shifted from Thaneswar to Kanauj; the Chalukyas ruled from Vatapi (Badami); Pallavas from Kanchipuram.

questions.question: During the post-Gupta period, which Tamil kingdom is known to have sent an embassy to the court of the Chinese Tang emperor and maintained maritime trade links?
questions.explanation: The Pallavas under Narasimhavarman I sent an embassy to China, and they had active trade relations with Southeast Asia and China.

questions.question: The 'Mandasor inscription' (473 CE) of the Gupta period records the construction of a temple by a guild of silk weavers. Which of the following inferences is most valid from this evidence?
options: Silk weaving was a royal monopoly in the Gupta Empire.
Guilds had considerable economic power and patronized religious structures.
The weavers were descendants of foreign migrants from the Roman Empire.
The temple was destroyed during the Hun invasions soon after.
questions.explanation: The inscription shows that occupational guilds were wealthy and influential enough to fund temple construction, indicating their socio-economic role.

questions.question: Which of the following post-Gupta developments had a direct impact on the evolution of Tamil Bhakti literature?
options: The spread of Vajrayana Buddhism from Bengal.
The construction of rock-cut cave temples by Pallava and Pandya kings.
The arrival of Muslim traders from Arabia on the Malabar coast.
The translation of Sanskrit Puranas into Tamil by court poets.
questions.explanation: The Pallava and Pandya patronage of temple construction and associated rituals (like the Tevaram and Divya Prabandham) directly fostered the Tamil Bhakti movement.

questions.question: Consider the following about the 'Arthashastra' and the Gupta-post-Gupta period: Which statement is correct?
options: The Arthashastra was composed during the Gupta period and reflected its administrative practices.
Kautilya's Arthashastra was lost and only rediscovered in the post-Gupta era.
Gupta rulers used the Arthashastra as a manual but modified its fiscal policies for the agrarian economy.
The Arthashastra was largely ignored during the Gupta period in favor of the Manusmriti.
questions.explanation: While the Arthashastra dates to the Mauryan period, Gupta rulers like Chandragupta II adopted its principles but adapted them to a feudal agrarian setting.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly links the post-Gupta period with the socio-religious reforms in Tamil Nadu?
options: The establishment of mathas by Shankaracharya in the 8th century CE.
The composition of the Kural by Tiruvalluvar under the patronage of the Pandya king.
The rise of Jain and Buddhist monastic orders in Madurai after the Kalabhra interregnum.
The conversion of Pallava king Mahendravarman I to Jainism.
questions.explanation: Shankaracharya's mathas (e.g., at Sringeri and Kanchi) were founded in the post-Gupta era and played a key role in the revival of orthodox Hinduism in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the 'Huna' invasions in the post-Gupta period is historically accurate?
options: The Huns destroyed the Gupta Empire completely, leaving no successor states.
Mihirakula, the Huna ruler, was finally defeated by the combined forces of the Guptas and the Maitrakas.
The Hun invasions were repelled by Skandagupta, but later waves under Toramana weakened the empire irreversibly.
The Hun invasions led to the immediate rise of the Pushyabhuti dynasty under Harsha.
questions.explanation: Skandagupta successfully repelled the early Hun attacks, but later under Toramana and Mihirakula, the Guptas lost control of western India, accelerating decline.
